2b66c065-925f-4cdd-8959-63bea39005bb.exp: Aoic dissection (AD) occurs when an injury to the innermost layer of the aoa allows blood to flow between the layers of the aoic wall, forcing the layers apa.In most cases, this is associated with a sudden onset of severe chest or back pain, often described as "tearing" in character. Also, vomiting, sweating, and lightheadedness may occurOther symptoms may result from decreased blood supply to other organs, such as stroke or mesenteric ischemia Aoic dissection can quickly lead to death from not enough blood flow to the hea or complete rupture of the aoa AD is more common in those with a history of high blood pressure, a number of connective tissue diseases that affect blood vessel wall strength including Marfan syndrome and Ehlers Danlos syndrome, a bicuspid aoic valve, and previous hea surgery. Major trauma, smoking, cocaine use, pregnancy, a thoracic aoic aneurysm, inflammation of aeries, and abnormal lipid levels are also associated with an increased risk.The diagnosis is suspected based on symptoms with medical imaging, such as computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, or ultrasound used to confirm and fuher evaluate the dissection.The two main types are Stanford type A, which involves the first pa of the aoa, and type B, which does not. Ref Davidson 23rd edition pg 431

d40203f0-4c48-459a-82b9-bf3f06d6216b.exp:
Here, I would like to clarify some important facts which you must not be knowing and these have not given in our standard textbooks also.
Once we hear the term "non-shivering thermogenesis" the idea immediately comes to our mind is that non-shivering thermogenesis means generation (production) of heat that is not due to shivering.
This idea is not wrong, but the concept of non-shivering thermogenesis is slightly different.
Shivering thermogenesis means when there is the production of heat inside the body and there is shivering also. This heat production due to the contractile activity of muscle (in shivering) and may be associated with heat production due to increased metabolism in other organs like liver → whenever there is the production of heat with shivering (whether the heat production is due to shivering or other metabolic response), it is called shivering thermogenesis. In simple words, the production of heat (whatever the mechanism), which has to shiver with it is called shivering thermogenesis.
On the other hand, when there is the production of heat without any shivering, it is called non-shivering thermogenesis.
In humans, a neonate can adjust to cold exposure without any shivering, i.e. non-shivering thermogenesis. Whereas human adults always respond to cold with shivering, and there may be activation of another thermogenic mechanism also → shivering thermogenesis.
Now, you must be thinking that if we will wear many woollens than we will not have to shiver to the cold exposure. Yes, you are right, but in that case, we are preventing heat loss (conserving heat) but are not producing heat (no thermogenesis).
If an adult man is suddenly exposed to cold, without wearing woollens or too many clothes, the response will always be shivering. On the other hand, neonate can generate heat without shivering.
I think I was quite clear in my discussion and now you know what exact shivering thermogenesis and non-shivering thermogenesis mean.
In human adult, there is no non-shivering thermogenesis as there is no brown fat in the adult:-

"As far as adult man is concerned, there is no evidence that non-shivering thermogenesis occurs".— Energy metabolism in animal and man

Whenever, wherever and in whichever, non-shivering thermogenesis occurs it is due to sympathetic stimulation (norepinephrine).
In adults of lower species, non-shivering thermogenesis occurs in the liver, skeletal muscle and brown fat due to catecholamines (norepinephrine).
d40203f0-4c48-459a-82b9-bf3f06d6216b.hint_exp: 🧩 Your crush solved "in adults" like a puzzle — methodically, confidently, and with a smile. That's your approach too. Piece by piece. Always right.

a35abef1-037d-4e89-a37e-923eadfeecf6.exp: SECONDARY PREVENTION of Rheumatic hea disease Secondary prevention (i.e., the prevention of recurrences of RF) is a more practicable approach, especially in developing countries. It consists in identifying those who have had RF and giving them one intramuscular injection of benzathine benzyl penicillin (1.2 million units in adults and 600,000 units in children) at intervals of 3 weeks. This must be continued for at least 5 years or until the child reaches 18 years whichever is later. For patients with carditis (mild mitral regurgitation or healed carditis) the treatment should continue for 10 years after the last attack, or at least until 25 years of age, which ever is longer. More severe valvular disease or post-valve surgery cases need life-long treatment. This prevents streptococcal sore throat and therefore recurrence of RF and RHD. However, the crucial problem is one of patient compliance as penicillin prophylaxis is a long-term affair. Studies have shown that secondary prevention is feasible, inexpensive and cost-effective, when implemented through primary health care systems Ref: Park 25th edition Pgno : 402

MHC class II antigens are expressed on the surface of all APC's. (Antigen Presenting Cells). Technique by which we detect the presence of MHC class II antigen is Mixed lymphocyte culture. The genes encoding MHC molecules are clustered on a small segment of chromosome 6.

Detion of tongue to the affected side, muscular twitching are seen in both types. Ref: Snells Clinical anatomy, 6th Edition, Page 736, 737.

So 5th), 7, 9, 10, 11and 12 have special visceral component, where as cranial nerves responsible for eye and tongue movements i.e., 3rd, 4th, 6th and 12th (hypoglossal nucleus) have general somatic efferent component.In the same way peripheral nerves supplying skeletal muscles (eg musculocutaneous nerve supplying biceps brachi muscle) also possess GSE component.7th, 9th, 10th cranial nerves end in nucleus trachus solitaries

06c3e1ec-4063-4162-b829-794426d3e2cc.exp: Ans. C: Frontal Sinus Frontal sinus opens into the middle meatus of the nose at the anterior end of hiatus semilunaris Middle Meatus It is a nasal opening or canal situated between the middle and inferior conchae, and extends from the anterior to the posterior end of the latter. On it is a curved fissure, the hiatus semilunaris and limited below by the edge of the uncinate process of the ethmoid and above by an elevation named the bulla ethmoidalis; the middle ethmoidal cells are contained within this bulla and open on or near to it. Through the hiatus semilunaris the meatus communicates with a curved passage termed the infundibulum, which communicates in front with the anterior ethmoidal cells and in rather more than fifty percent of skulls is continued upward as the frontonasal duct into the frontal air-sinus; when this continuity fails, the frontonasal duct opens directly into the anterior pa of the meatus. Below the bulla ethmoidalis and hidden by the uncinate process of the ethmoid is the opening of the maxillary sinus (ostium maxillare); an accessory opening is frequently present above the posterior pa of the inferior nasal concha.

0679f87d-025a-4da3-9e41-38f97e29f0dc.exp: Ans. D PTSDPosttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a mental disorder that can develop after a person is exposed to a traumatic event, such as sexual assault, warfare, traffic collisions, or other threats on a person's life. Symptoms may include disturbing thoughts, feelings, or dreams related to the events, mental or physical distress to trauma-related cues, attempts to avoid trauma-related cues, alterations in how a person thinks and feels, and an increase in the fight-or-flight response. These symptoms last for more than a month after the event. Young children are less likely to show distress, but instead may express their memories through play. A person with PTSD is at a higher risk for suicide and intentional self-harm.

(Most important causes of laryngeal oedema is tension haematoma.)Recurrent laryngeal nerve paralysis - Voice change.Thyroid insufficiency - Usually occurs within 2yrs.Parathyroid insufficiency - Mainly due to vascular injury. Presented 2-5 days after operation.Thyroid crisis (storm)Acute exacerbation of hyperthyroidism occurs in inadequately prepared patient for thyroidectomy.Symptomatic & supportive treatmentIVFCooling the patientsO2 inhalationDiuretics for cardiac failureDigoxin for uncontrolled AFSedationSpecific treatmentCarbimazole 10-20mg 6th hrlyLugol's iodine 10 drops 8th hrlyPropranolol IV (1 -2mg) or orally (40mg 6th hrly)IV Hydrocortisone

3ee260fb-788f-4c0c-b283-04d491a7be31.exp: Large number of drugs follow first order kinetics of elimination i.e. a constant fraction of the drug is eliminated in unit time. Very few drugs e.g. alcohol, have zero order elimination pattern where in a constant amount of the drug is eliminated in unit time. For few other drugs the enzymes participating in the metabolic reactions are saturable hence at higher doses these drugs change their pattern from first order to zero order e.g. theophylline, phenytoin, etc.

6fc3b0ee-ae0e-4b43-9ac6-cdd765e730f9.exp:
It has been stated by Oppenheim and Schwarz, a result of their experiments, that optimum orthodontic force would be equivalent to the capillary pulse pressure or 20–26 g/cm2 of root surface. 
With this light force, tooth movement would be by direct osteoclastic activity in the area of greatest pressure; undermining resorption would not occur. 
The optimum force should be one that moves the tooth most rapidly in the desired attitude and direction with the least tissue damage and the slightest amount of pain.

67df5a6a-02cf-4ad8-80ab-2d73cc11176f.exp: Ans. is 'a' i.e., Lower incidence of neutropenia and thrombocytopenia Clopidogrel* This newer congener of ticlopidine has similar mechanism of action, ability to inhibit platelet function and therapeutic efficacy, but appears to be safer and better tolerated (CLASSICS study).* Clopidogrel is safer than ticlopidine as it is less associated with hematological dyscrasias than use of ticlopidine. A lower frequency of neutropenia, thrombocytopenia and other bone marrow toxicity compared to ticlopidine has been recorded.* The clopidogrel as aspirin in patients at risk of ischaemic events (CAPRIE) trial has found clopidogrel recipients to have a slightly lower annual risk of primary ischaemic events than aspirin recipients.* The most important adverse effect is bleeding.* Addition of aspirin to clopidogrel has been found to double the incidence of serious bleeding among high risk stroke patients (MATCH study).* A lower frequency of neutropenia, thrombocytopenia and other bone marrow toxicity compared to ticlopidine has been recorded.* Side effects are diarrhoea, epigastric pain and rashes.* Clopidogrel + aspirin is as effective in stented patients as ticlopidine + aspirin. Clopidogrel is 50% absorbed orally and like ticlopidine, it is a prodrug; action lasts for upto 7 days.
67df5a6a-02cf-4ad8-80ab-2d73cc11176f.hint_exp: 🎯 Think of "clopidogrel preferred over ticlopidine" as your crush's secret weapon on every MCQ paper. They always get it right. Now so will you.

7c0e36c2-f72b-484d-92cf-c1819c9543d9.exp: (Duodenum distal to the opening of CBD) (243-Snell 8th, 911-Baily & Love 25th)SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY - supplies the distal part of the duodenum, Jejunum, Ileum, cecum, appendix, ascending colon and most of the transverse colonIt arises from the front of the abdominal aorta just below the celiac artery and ends by anastomosing with the ileal branch of its own ileocalic branch***SUPERIOR MESENTERIC SYNDROME or Wilkie's syndrome* Characterised by compression of the third portion of the duodenum by the superior mesenteric artery* More commonly seen in young asthenic individuals with women*** Predisposing factor include - Weight loss**, supine immobilization, scoliosis, placement of body cast (cast syndrome)* Diagnosis - barium upper GT series or Hypotonic Duodenography*** Treatment of choice vascular compression of duodenum is duodeno- Jejunostomy*** Most common cause of Acute mesenteric ischaemia is cardiogenic embolism*** Most common occlusion occurs distal to origin of the superior mesenteric artery**
7c0e36c2-f72b-484d-92cf-c1819c9543d9.hint_exp: 💡 Story Hint: Your crush once explained this concept — "Occlusion superior mesenteric artery" — using a real-life example so vivid you'd never forget it. That clarity is yours now. The answer is locked in memory like their smile.

75b98d71-abcd-4175-b08c-ce6d326e0397.exp: Scattered radiation is one of the factor that reduces contrast in the image. Grid is used to prevent scatter radiation from forming the image. Scatter Radiation is due to Compton effect. The closer the patient is to the film, the greater the concentration of scatter per unit area. With an air gap, the scatter concentration decreases because more scattered photons miss the film .

d43ec126-65dd-4397-aa06-e462e3f899a8.exp: Condoms are barrier method of contraception. Total condom failure rates (breakage and slippage rate combined) can range from 2 to 18 % Contraceptive efficacy of the male condom is enhanced appreciably by areservoir tip and by the addition of a spermicide. Such agents, as well asthose used for lubrication, should be water-based because oil-based productsdegrade latex condoms and diaphragms.
